commentBody:
Zhou said it for me:
"Obviously, we have a perfect understanding of day-of-week difficulty, so when we wrote this we confidently clued it at a Wednesday level, only to have it be accepted for a Thursday."

Nice one, Zhou and Mal. IMO, it has legs.

Love this puzzle. I keep forgetting to look at constructor names before starting, but I've loved all their collaborations. 

Love the theme, including the discarded ones in constructor notes, though I don't quite get how a glass of wine has drippy legs?

More proof that it is not the constructors who are trying to water down Thursdays. 
I have great respect for Mallory and Zhou as constructors and this is no exception, this would have been a great Wednesday or even Tuesday as clued. Some of the clues were very fresh especially in the bottom half, but for the most part I thought they were very straightforward even for a Wednesday, much less Thursday. And this theme was fun but screamingly obvious from the first entry. Again, nothing wrong with this at all for earlier in the week, but I refuse to believe that the traditional Thursday style puzzles have disappeared from the editor’s submission queue, so what is going on?

When I saw that Zhou Zhang had co-constructed this puzzle, I thought that I wouldn’t have a leg to stand on.  Turns out I needn’t have worried, because this delightful puzzle was not what I had simultaneously feared and hoped for.  Fie on the NYT for slotting this on Thursday rather than Wednesday as the constructors had anticipated.  I enjoyed it well enough, but couldn’t help but feel a little disappointed.

“Mince words?” for edit was a nice touch for a common crossword entry.
As a test solver for this puzzle, I remember my delight when I first solved it and my hope that it would be published. Congrats, Mallory and Zhou, lovely people who construct lively crosswords!
Maybe I'm tired, but I solved in near record time without ever catching on to the gimmick. I guess this was an easier puzzle for a Thursday.

Fun puzzle today.  Good overall theme, some frustrating parts.

I did a Half Ironman last weekend so it was nice to immediately see IRONMAN TRIATHLON, and it's great that was the clue that made me figure out the theme.

There's a few clues I just do not get even after getting them with crossings.  SMASH for 'Sensation'.  What do those two words have to do with each other?  And OX TEAM felt a bit weak just because although my first thought was OX I've never heard that full expression.  Also never heard of El CID, or AVER for 'Attest'.

My biggest sticking point was that my first guess for the Frosty was DENNYS and it has enough letters in common with WENDYS that it threw me off for a while.

So good puzzle but a lot of really questionable filler clues.

It was hard for me until it wasn’t. 
I definitely knew I needed to crack the revealer first. Once that fell, so did the puzzle. 

Well well below average solve time. But creative. 
I love clues like “scratch that!”  I fall for it every time. 

I always thought the big hand was the fat / short one. And the tall / skinny hand was called the little hand. Not sure why. Don’t make any sense. But I promise I can read time.

I've flyspecked longer than I filled in squares, and I just cannot find the error.

I am trying to ease myself into being really disturbed by whatever is wrong, because I feel really confident about everything.

This is going to be one of those easy puzzles with one killer cross.
Fun puzzle!

Yes, and they were considered a “major Hollywood landmark” (11)
.
.
.
BETTYGRABLE
Anyone else think that, eh, DEVILE is probably a word and move on? Took me ages to find that error.

if a millipede took a spa day it would get a many-pedi. 

Gotta admit, solved without getting the theme til the revealer but still had a blast, and though it's not like Zhou's last partnering with Mr. Curry, it's still a sparkler. Liked the Japanese triad of Asahi, Noh and roe on the chirashi bowl, maybe sauce should be included, too. 

I remember going into a bookstore where their zoology section was arranged by number of legs: no legs (worms, snakes), one legged -- gastropods (literally stomach-foot) -- snails and the like* -- two-legged, us bipeds and a few other anthro types, quadrupeds, insects, 8-legged arachnids (which were ranked with the "many legged," the proprietor having played out the joke perhaps too far already), etc. 

*Pliny the Elder described a mythical race of people,  Sciapods, with a single enormous leg and foot, who supposedly lay on their backs and used the giant feet as shade against the sun. 

Not too many three legged creatures out there, although the Sicilian flag has the image of a Triskelion, a three-legged being representing the three headlands of the island. And my wife tells me that kangaroos often use their tail as a third leg, pushing off with it.

So I know Sanaa is the capital of YEMEN but I just couldn’t retrieve that fact.  I knew it would come to me eventually but to save time I Googled it. Does that count as cheating? I want to put it to a vote.  I didn’t have any other lookups.
Great, fun, puzzle. Personally, I don't care what day of the week it runs on, as long as it has legs and runs.

How about today’s Midi? With all the New England states where they belong in relation to each other? Incredibly clever! You have to admire feats like that, especially within the constraints of a Midi. Bravo!
Can someone explain 6D to me?

By the way, for those of you who experienced my wrath at a NYT puzzle a few days ago with an APIASAMOA crossed with SAPOR...

Apia, Samoa was an answer in a recent New Yorker puzzle.

It was like the editors at the New Yorker saw my screed, and decided to tease me by using it in their puzzle.

First Thursday in July that isn't the worst one of the year. 
So that's something to appreciate. And I do.

ESPAÑA practically front and center in the puzzle.
From the grid's lips to God's ears!

I was hoping for a World Cup final between England and Spain, but here we are. (Very sad...)

With England's loss, I'll be all in for Spain in the final match. 

You see, I'm half-Brazilian and the rivalry is real – a Brazilian never ever roots for Argentina! That would be a capital offense, an unadmissable betrayal. 
So, come Sunday, I'll be, brazenly, in my Brazil shirt, which translates to  more than just supporting Spain; for those who know, it screams "no" to Argentina taking home the trophy. ;-)

But back to the puzzle... It didn’t have Thursday trickery but it had smarts, and I loved it. 

Between clues and entries, we got LEGS, EYE, BUTTS, feet, and hand. Nice!

Thank you, Zhou and Mallory for a SMASHing puzzle. 
You really are excellent constructors and your puzzles are a joy to solve.
Please don't make us wait too long for the next one!

Intricately clued and cleverly put together.  Sometimes seemed easy, sometimes seemed impenetrable, but never boring, with no rebuses necessary to trip us up.  A puzzle with LEGS!

Thank you, Zhou and Mallory, for keeping us on our TOEs.
I found today’s photo very apt, as I spent far too long trying dog wearables for the lab clue. In a crossword, lab always means the dog. Always! 😂

A further comment on 14D. In earlier days, when knights were being created, before being dubbed they were literally anointed, blessed with sacred oil. 
It only later that the metonymic usage of “anointed” to the whole process arose.

Am I the only gutter dweller whose memories of… a guy named Art, or Bob, or Stew… if the answer to this puzzle’s question is ‘No’?

Gosh we used to be crude and insensitive.

Re 60A - neither dictionary I checked (m-w.com, ahdictionary.com) supports the given clue ("Raving") as a definition for AVID.

And "Worked sufferingly" seems like a curiously worded clue for 52D (TOILED).

I’m 39 years old and have told time on an analog clock for as long as I remember, and ALWAYS thought the hand pointing to the hour was called the “big” hand and the minute hand the “little”! I even explained it recently to my 5 year old and added that it’s called the big hand even though it’s shorter than the other (but usually thicker!) and my husband didn’t correct me. Am I alone in this and have been wrong my whole life?!

The Wordplay column states: "The clue at 18-Across replies [*Yes — three arduous ones]. Its answer is IRONMAN TRIATHLON, the grueling athletic event that consists of three legs: a 2.4-mile swim, a 112-mile cycling ride and a marathon."

Why anyone would want to do this is beyond me. But I assume, if you can accomplish it, that's pretty amazing. I would imagine that the athlete needs a lifetime of training, and probably has to give up almost everything else, to be able to succeed at this.

Count me out. No matter how many legs I had.

My screen had the right-hand column off the page.  Only visible in list view. I thought this was the trick of this puzzle.  Also, letter squares were hard to zero in on.  It became necessary to scroll up and down list view to insert specific letters.

“I don’t think these answers will shave legs, Clem.”

Anti-hoi polloi comment quota reached.  Have fun!

I had hex instead of POX and strive instead of STRIFE, so that slowed me down a bit. A tiny observation on 17A: If Einstein was right about the nature of the universe (and who am I to argue), time is part of the fabric of the universe itself and doesn’t exist independently of it. So asking what came “before” the universe doesn’t make sense - time (as we know it) started with the universe and there is no “before”. But for crossword purposes I’ll accept the clue ;-)

Great puzzle, just chewy enough for me. I must say I expected a plural answer for 49D, the clue being plural; that had me briefly stymied. But the crosses filled in what I thought was correct, if not plural, so I ran with it.

I'm not currently at home in Massachusetts (I'm at home in France, where there are also wildfires), but if you are under yellow skies, stay indoors as much as you can to protect your lungs.

Good for a Thursday. I want to put in a comment here about another NYT game. Connections. I have stopped playing it because it really has no logic and is only dependent on us guessing what's inside the mind of the constructor. It's fiendish and frustrating.

I agree with an earlier post about the disconnect between “Raving” and “Avid”.  Similarly, close by I don’t think I’d connect “yaps at” with “harangue”.  Being yapped at is a lot weaker and more pointless than  enduring a harangue.

As usual, Zhou and Mallory's humor shines throughout this puzzle.  The outlier at 50 A with it's groan worthy clue wrapped up the theme with a bow on top.

And, Zhou, you were on the right track.  You just haven't adjusted your feel for day of week difficulty to the standards of 

The Great Easening 

period in puzzling at the NYT.
Really clever and funny, thank you! LOVE that LOLCATS made an entry. And now I’m hungry; I CAN HAS CHEEZBURGER?

Good puzzle. I kept looking for the tricksy rebi, but no.
As I began to write that I didn’t understand POSH for smart, it hit me. Duh.

Hex before POX. I see I’m in good company with that error. Not familiar with SAUCE for alcohol but ok.

So. The football. Talk about snatching defeat from the jaws of victory, why oh why did Tuchel flood the field with defenders? 
The only other thing I have to say is that the other team are not gentlemen. Yes, the two goals were magnificent and well played, but the behaviour otherwise was appalling. The less said about the banner they produced afterwards the better. We’ll all be behind ESPANA on Sunday. Viva ESPANA.

[Yes – apparently, but it’s difficult to imagine]



SEA

Nice. I liked the theme. For me, the top half was pretty easy, but I started to go astray at 'patooties' which I didn't know, and at the Japanese bowl - I've never had any Japanese food. 

I've never heard of a SMOCK in the lab, only a lab coat. I agree that YAPS AT is a bit dodgy. 

Overall though, very enjoyable.
Had a devil of a time sorting out some incorrect guesses, but in the end it was a very enjoyable solve.

I thoroughly enjoyed this. 
Got a bit tripped up in the 
AVID/AVow/AVER, 
Edens/ETHER area,

but otherwise it was smooth sailing.

I loved that the themers were (half) befuddling,
and the revealer was an uber-cool spanner.

I believe both constructors
*can*
has cheezburger.

Indubitably.

Well, how playful is that, where you have to figure out the question that the theme answers’ clues answer, in addition to figuring out the theme answers themselves.  Did my riddle-loving brain like that?  Oh, you bet it did.

Not to mention uncovering lovely answers IN THE BAG, SMOCK, ANOINT, and RAKE IT IN.

This pair of constructors revels in fun and silly, and so do I, and so I revel in this pair of constructors.

A serendipitous theme echo in the puzzle: there are at least six answers, such as OX TEAM and HANDEL, that yes, also have legs.

Nice to see that after being shorted a column on Tuesday, we’re given an extra one today.  

Zhou and Mallory, I leave your creative and entertaining puzzles uplifted, happy, bouncing out of the room.  You bring joy to the box. Thank you!
Very silly, which is just fine with me.
I don’t get MIN.
I enjoy puzzles that are deliberately, unapologetically obfuscatory. Kinda the point innit?

A perfectly solid Tuesday puzzle, with accessible fill and an easily guessed theme and revealer. No idea why this appeared on a Thursday. Done in about fifteen minutes, and didn't even have to work for it. So, somehow disappointing ... again.

Was a bit surprised to see the same preposition in both the clue and answer for 39D: "Roll in dough" for RAKEITIN. I suppose the clue is an attempt to misdirect us toward baking, but I found the clue-answer pair just a bit jarring. Maybe just "Gather dough" would have been better.

Also, haranguing is much more forceful than mere yapping!
IMHO the clue to 1 across is incorrect.  The meaning of the clue is rich and elegant, not merely smart. For example, a crisp white shirt with a pair of black jeans is smart, but not xxxx.

LEGendary! (Has someone already posted that?)

Super creative and fun puzzle.

These constructors got legs and they know how to use them! (Has someone already posted that?)

Thanks to Sean Mc Gowan.  Today I learned the distinction between endonyms and exonyms.  I've felt the need to know such terms for a long time, and I hope I will remember the words.  
I've always thought it was strange when American reporters would pronounce the names of certain countries as if they were natives of that land (Nicaragua comes to mind), but were perfectly happy to pronounce others (Germany, France, Italy, Greece, Israel) in the American fashion.  Did they want their audience to know that they had studied Spanish in High School?

As for today's puzzle: it was fine.  It produced a couple of interesting stumbling blocks for me.  But it wasn't Thursday-worthy.
It's hard to imagine someone asking of a MILLIPEDE, "DOES THIS HAVE LEGS?"  
..or a DIRECT FLIGHT, for that matter.
Oh well, that's the problem with trying to come up with an original theme, I guess.

The constructors say: "Actually, we keep submitting Wednesdays that end up running on Thursdays."

We noticed.

This is a conscious editorial decision to run what should have been a Tuesday or Wednesday puzzle on a Thursday. I understand if there is a dearth of Thursday tricks, as these are probably pretty hard to think up. But the difficulty level of this puzzle is not appropriate for the latter half of the week.

Thank you to Zhou Zhang and Mallory Montgomery for a lovely puzzle. And to the editors: Do better.
After fifteen minutes of getting almost nowhere I found myself thinking, "Wait, this is Thursday. Here we go again."  And then as if by magic things began to fall into place.

Whew. Typical long Thursday workout for me, but as usual, finally tumbling to the theme was the big turning point. Really clever theme and it ended up being a quite enjoyable solve. And... one puzzle find today was inspired by one of the constructor's name. Wondered about ZH...ZH in any answer and... yep - ZHUZHUP was the reveal in a Wednesday puzzle from last year. Some other theme answers in that one: TAMPABEIGE SKIPTOMYLUGE THEDEADSIEGE BUCKAROUGE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/3/2025&h=38a Might put another puzzle in a reply. ....

I, too, am outraged that there is normal deviation around the Thursday average difficulty!  All seriousness aside, while I solved this faster than my Thursday average, I was slower than my Wednesday average.  Anyway, who cares, it was a fun one!

Yesterday's puzzle had a thread in which one solver questioned my idea that Rubic's Cube did not have much "play value," asking if any toys in fact did have a point  (after which one wag noted that a Lawn Dart definitely had a point.....tsk)...  I belatedly answer:

@Elbridge Gerry
Almost all of them!!  (Not the one below!)  Do you somehow think that joy, fun, and play are not important in life, in child-rearing, in learning???  Yikes.
My multi-handicapped class loved learning "Go Fish."  They were practicing taking turns, *paying attention*, using recall, losing gracefully, and numeral identification (there being multiple levels of  education/ability.)  The aides LOVED coming in to assist; many of them had never heard of the game (!) and came back the next week saying their family loved it (even all adult-groupings) ...  Games and toys (and yes, I categorize them together) afford many learning opportunities for all kids!

I was way ahead of this puzzle, theme-wise -- in a way that made me feel smart.  I love it when a constructor can make me such an active part of the solving process.

I had filled in IRONMAN TRIATHLON.  As soon as I got to the MILL of MILLIPEDE, I knew that 1) It would be MILLIPEDE and that 2) the revealer would be DOES IT HAVE LEGS.  After that, the clues for DIRECT FLIGHT and YARDSTICK both confirmed it.  (I don't understand the "in common usage" aspect of the DIRECT FLIGHT clue and maybe someone can explain it?)

If I'd been really brilliant, I would have seen the DIRECT FLIGHT and YARDSTICK answers just from the clues and with very few crosses.  I wasn't that brilliant, but maybe some of you were.

Nothing in the puzzle gave me any trouble.  But there was a different kind of thinking to do and I had an enjoyable time doing it.  The grid is delightfully free of crosswordese and pop culture.  I appreciate that so much -- especially after yesterday.  

One word, though, about Jane EYRE.  She seems to be the most oft-quoted protagonist in literature.  Is that because everything she said is so interesting, so trenchant?   As far as I'm concerned, she's pretty dull. Reader -- I skimmed her!   But the arrangement of letters in her last name have a pattern that would seem to be invaluable to grid-makers.

A very entertaining puzzle with the theme answers clued in a way that's very entertaining.
Good fun.  Managed to guess the other 2 themers but could not make the connection to DIRECTFLIGHT and needed the crosses to get it.   Nice one and thanks.

As someone who does the puzzle diligently every day of the week, I implore others to avoid getting hung up on the exact difficulty level of a “___day” puzzle.  

Better to enjoy each one just as it is, and look forward to the next as it comes!
EYRE gave me the EYE and that finished the grid. Versatile, ain't she?

Nice one. Liked the constructor notes, too. Have these or any other constructors ever considered using Quetzalcoatl as a seed entry? (Whew! I had Saved that word from their notes and for a bit there it wouldn't Paste here.)

Good morning! Completed the puzzle, and then got the theme. Plenty of fun! (Would have started off with "chic", but 1-4 down made it clear.) 
Every day with a puzzle is a happy day! PS totally bombed on Connections - fell for all the misdirects. 
Well, Scarlett, tomorrow is another day!
Glad there were no rebuses. I really don't like them.
Gasoline on the "the puzzles are getting too easy" fire. I just finished this Friday from the archives: https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2014/11/14
LOVE NYT crosswords.....but is there no WSJ crossword today?

🎉 A grid from my favorite team of constructors! Zhou and Mallory brought the smiles and giggles into my home once again 🤣 Such a clever theme and  YARDSTICK was truly dad joke level groan worthy 😆
We have stayed at the Waikoloa Hilton on the Big Island a number of times and watched both the IRONMAN TRIATHLON and the Lavaman finish lines there. Truly incredible athletes! We were in Coeur d’Alene in June for that Ironman as well. We never actually plan to go see these competitions, it’s always coincidental that our stay coincides. Impeccable timing I guess! 
Rooting for Team ESPAÑA…we travelled throughout Spain in 2012 for two weeks. We watched the final game at our hotel in Pamplona as they won the Euro Cup. We moved on to Madrid the following day and our hotel was along the victory parade route down to the fountain. We happened to be in Nuremberg, Germany in 2014 staying with a former exchange student that we hosted when Germany won the World Cup. They deemed us the “lucky charms” 🤣
On a side note, my daughter is off to Camp Minikani in Wisconsin this weekend. Her group is set to do a 30 mile hike of the Superior Trail in Minnesota between Beaver Bay and Silver Bay next week. I understand the air quality is hazardous at this point. After seeing Francis’s post, I’m really concerned about our northern neighbors and friends on this forum. Stay safe!!

Didn't get DOES THIS HAVE LEGS until after I figured out the answers to the question. "Brainstorming session" seems a little too vague and even completely unrelated to any brainstorming session I've been a part of. Also, I still don't understand SHE-goat. Is that akin to a werewolf of sorts?
Awesome Tuesday puzzle!
Feb.14th is usually day Pitchers and Catchers report to Spring Training. That’s my Valentine.

I rise in heartfelt praise of the little word. Let us all hail and laud the three or four letter easy answer in any grid. Today's example was three letters, Japanese drama, and that one I know. 
For oh! On so many a morning I'll encounter some lengthy thematic answer with wordplay and/or trickery I haven't decoded, and then another and another; and just when I'm about to despair and give up crosswords for a career in restoring Victrola hand-cranked record-players, I will move elsewhere in the puzzle to find those gettable answers, often deliberately quite easy, to balance the puzzle difficulty, and all order will be restored to my universe. The sun will emerge from behind clouds, cats will stop chasing dogs, up will again be down; and I will find a beacon of hope that if I fill in enough "ENO" and "UMA" answers, I will soon enough be ready for a renewed attack on the hard stuff. 
Thank you, you overlooked and undersung fill. You are the tempeh in my la la.   Praise! All join me in praising the undersung "GRE" and "ATE" answers, humble and pure in their intentions and noble in their service. 
Praise and hail!

Actually, I just like to say SMOCK. 
SMOCK, SMOCK, SMOCK!
(Per Calvin and Hobbes)
Very forgiving Thursday, after the last two.
The 50 Across clue solve would have been nice to be "grand piano" :-)

poem made from words in today’s puzzle…

a/ the day that preceded the big bang, what was that like? 

george says life is a lonesome question—george, who is the last of his bloodline…

and yes who knows what the tortoise knows — like was that tortoise sometimes happy? and who knows what lonesome george knows of life when life preceded the big bang?

d/ wait… really?
really a/ no one?
Great puzzle to run on any other day. Embarrassing that this is what the editors decided to offer on a Thursday.
5-across? I’m not familiar with that use of the word! Can anyone ’splain?
39D uses the word "in" for both the hint and the answer. Isn't that against the rules?

Overall enjoyable and the theme actually helped me solve it, but a couple of nits. I wouldn’t consider POSH as Smart; chic is a more apt answer. IMO, posh is associated with wealth or upper classes, and as we know from the madman in the White House, wealth can often be gaudy, not smart. And I don’t get the connection between sensation and SMASH, except in the most general of ways.

I’m not convinced “struggle” and “strife” are proper synonyms. 

They are seldom interchangeable and have sufficiently distinct meanings — or connotations at least. 

Examples:

The parties were engaged in a struggle/strife for power

There was a long history of strife/struggle between the nations 

He found it a struggle/strife to understand calculus


As verbs, they are much more closely related. One cannot easily substitute “strive” for “struggle”. As nouns? Not so much. I don’t care what Google, AI or OED have to say about it.
By the time I got there, I instantly figured the lab in question was a dog. Thank goodness I already had a cross in place that didn’t fit with “leash”
As a middle aged father of two, I approve of this puzzle. I never turn my nose up at a good play on words.
I never used SAUCE as a term for alcohol, so I was completely confused. I know it to mean "swag" so I learned something new :)
Maddening at first, but as I tried my own word games I started to get it.  I ended up having a great time with this one!
Such a fun theme! Smiled all the way through.

This was a tough but manageable solve. I loved the theme; tough but fair. The SW was my biggest challenge.

Rating:
8/10

I wouldn't say ROE is a garnish in a chirashi bowl - it's one of its actual ingredients
.

AVID = RAVING?
Huh?

Yes it has legs! I enjoyed the puzzle and agree it’s more like a Wednesday but it was fun and I had to work a bit. I wondered what kind of lab it is where people wear smocks—I worked in a lab for some years and we wore lab coats and goggles etc. and always a badge. I think of smocks for finger-painting and the like. I first entered “badge” since it fit but that didn’t last. Anyway, the theme was clever and fun. Thank you.
I forgot to mention: “lugubrious” is one of my favorite words.
I don't get hung up on day-of-week expectations but the relative scarcity of really challenging ones (on any day) over recent months does bum me out a bit. (Still loved this one though!)

So much fun, and quite challenging enough for my taste. I actually finished it faster than expected because I hadn't quite figured out the theme when I was suddenly startled by the happy music. I went back through the whole thing and then saw the light.
Thanks Zhou and Mallory. Looking forward to your next collaboration.
I sometimes dread the Thursday puzzles, but this one was delightful!

I never met Elizabeth I, but had occasion to, and regret immensely that I didn’t make more of an effort. She was visiting our village in the Midlands in the Summer of 1574 as part of a massive two year-long Royal Tour across the nation. In those days the highest honor that could be bestowed upon a rural hamlet such as ours was a visit from the Sovereign, and believe me, all the stops were pulled in preparation for the event. Unfortunately, it was two-for-one taco night at the pub the evening before her arrival, and I hit that scene hard, my brothers. I mean, I had like 37 barbacoa and 24 carnitas if you know what I’m saying. So when I awoke the next morning, there was definitely a situation going on. A situation which, regrettably, precluded my being anywhere other than glued to the chamber pot all day.  As I heard the herald trumpets sound their majestic fanfare across the dell, and the roar of the adoring subjects as Her Highness arrived, I alone remained in bed, all aquiver and glistening with meat sweats, with what can only be described as an unusually acute and unforgiving case of “the green apple splatters.”  I’ve been uniquely fortunate in my 620 year lifetime to have met some of the most distinguished personages throughout history, but I may be the only man who can honestly claim he failed to meet a queen because he had to drop a DEUCE.

I like this one but it was definitely tougher for me than it sounds like it was for many of you, which is fine. I finished up about 30% lower than my average. 

Because it just couldn't be my mind working a little slower than normal (ETHER, no! Perish the very thought!) I'm going to blame it on all the smoke. 

I normally go to campus on Thursdays, but I tried to water the plants on our garden balcony and thought I was going to keel over, so I worked from home. But that tiny bit of smoke inhalation is clearly what affected my average time here. Har!! 

Once I figured the theme out, I got a good chuckle out of it. I hate corporate speak, so it's fun to mock it!

Definitely not on the same wavelength as the constructors. The theme was kind of fun but for me some of the clue/answers were a bit clunky. I've worked in many labs and never heard of SMOCK being used, only lab coat. A SMOCK for me is an art studio attire. YAPSAT is a much weaker synonym for harangues imho. One of a kind did not register ACE, there is more than one in a deck of cards and while top notch experts may be rare there's usually more than one. Finally, a walking bass line is very strongly associated with jazz and the blues but I have never heard it mentioned in the same breath as SKA. Again I appreciate the constructors efforts and I thought the theme was fun, the awkwardness was a real detraction for me.
Enjoyed the theme but got stuck on 26A. I had DEvILE which I knew was not a word. It took too long for me to realize 8D was STRIFE instead of STRIvE.

This one was an odd one for me. I started it knowing it was a Thursday and expecting it to be tricky. But starting in the NW and working my way down, I filled in the first several entries fairly quickly and easily. I started feeling remorseful for Thursday puzzles really having a shockingly low level of difficulty now. 

Then I got into the middle and bottom parts of the puzzle. Much harder, with much more of a challenge. What the heck was I thinking before? I loved finally figuring out what the starred clues meant. Very enjoyable experience for me. 

Very interesting to hear from the constructors Zhou and Mallory that they themselves thought it to be a Wednesday-level puzzle. They certainly didn’t consider it a Tricky Thursday. Here’s hoping that that practice isn’t gone for good. What a shame that would be.
Betty Grable puzzle today.

I really enjoyed a lot of the clues in this puzzle ! The focus was on 
words and real word meanings.... some humor woven in as well 
Nice work !!
What a big hand indicates…MIN? Please explain

Not sure why people think these puzzles are so easy.  I am reliably a bit under my average most days, but no where near my best times.  Maybe I should start these earlier in the day, after a good sleep.  I don't google anything so there is a lot of guesswork getting started.  I am grateful for the occasional trivia that I NOH already.
@Barry Ancona I finished it, but it took 50% longer than my Wednesday average.

@Barry Ancona

Your go-to site Xwstats rates it "Average." I'd say slipping from Wednesday to Thursday is no big deal.

(Oozing, not dripping)

(My memory has legs, that's why it tends to wander off)

@Isabeau The drops or streaks on the inside of the wine glass after it is swirled are sometimes called legs, sometimes tears. How they move has to do with the difference in how alcohol and water evaporate and also how sweet or high in alcohol the wine is.

@Isabeau

When you swirl the glass
then stop and see the wine
gliding down in stripes
or “legs”

it’s apparently indicative of higher alcohol content,
though though some consider a way 
distinguish a quality vino.

@CCNY
Wine enthusiasts are quick to insist legs don't tell you anything about quality; however, the thickness and the speed at which they drip down can offer clues about alcohol and sugar content.

@john ezra
My dog Sandy had 3 legs, but she didn’t start out that way.  And she didn’t have any books written about her.

@john ezra

While we’re on the topic of odd mythological stories, Aristophanes in the “Symposium” described early humans as spherical with four legs and four arms and two faces looking in opposite directions. When they became too arrogant, Zeus split them in two and now we are constantly searching for our “other half” and clinging to them desperately once we encounter them.
Here's a link to the wikipedia article on monopods, with extensive remarks on Pliny's Sciapods. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monopod_(creature)
@SP, lots of Jungian possibilities in there, the splitting of anima and animus and our constant striving to unite them...

@john ezra
That reminds me of when I was in graduate school many years ago and on a whim I kept my collection of books arranged by color. My fellow students and friends didn't notice or didn't admit to noticing. I was rather adept at finding books by color ("Gravitation" was black, "Classical Electrodynamics" was green but its 2nd edition was red, "Topology" was blue, etc.). Today those texts are somewhat lost amongst the many colored books that I now have. I'm really beginning to appreciate digital format.
@john ezra. We do have “the three legs of Man”, see the Manx flag

@Keith

"Gravitation"!!  I had that book.  

I couldn't even get started--I consoled myself by telling myself that I was only a lowly chemist.

I never knew anyone who did actually read it, or even a part of it, but a lot of people sure had it on their shelves.
@john ezra A lovely disquisition on a lovely puzzle.
@john ezra C. S. Lewis's monopods were the ones with which I was familiar. Like Pliny's they lie on their backs with the foot in the air.

@john ezra 
I had never heard of the Sciapods; clearly the inspiration for the Dufflepuds in "The Voyage of the Dawn Treader" from The Chronicles of Narnia.

@john ezra 

This sounds exactly like the Dufflepuds in "The Voyage of the Dawn Treader" from C.S. Lewis' "Chronicles of Narnia."  I wonder if the Sciapods were his inspiration?  I'm betting so, his classic knowledge of mythology would seem to make this more than a coincidence.

Glad to know about the connection of the Sciapods to CS Lewis's Dufflepuds in Narnia. Definitely a strong connection between the two. And also glad to know about the Triskelion on the Manx flag: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flag_of_the_Isle_of_Man

@Wayne Harrison

You are the only vote that counts.  But...

when I google anything I consider it a blue star at best.

But I don't judge.

@Wayne Harrison
You can treat each clue as an answer as in the Jeopardy game show and if the host accepts your answer, fine, and lookups are not allowed. However you could allow yourself to do some things that don't cause the app to not let you get the gold star and lookups, for example, would be fine. Whatever your standards are...

@Wayne Harrison. When I was in high school in the 1980s, I very much wanted to be an educated person, so I tried to do things educated people did, like listen to WQXR and do the Sunday NYT crossword puzzle. Using all the resources in my house (dictionary, thesaurus, encyclopedia, atlas) on a really good Sunday I could complete a quarter of the puzzle. Then I left for college and didn’t try the puzzle again until March 2020, when I suddenly had some time on my hands. Much to my surprise, after a few months I could often do Sunday puzzles without help. But if I google something I could have looked up in the paper resources in my childhood home, I don’t count it as cheating. So I am granting you a gold star for today, as I could have found Sanaa in the atlas or encyclopedia. (Just my personal rule.)
@Wayne Harrison I don’t consider educating oneself as cheating. I think it’s part of the puzzle.
@Wayne HarrisonYou could also have looked it up in the answer key. Same thing.
@Wayne Harrison your puzzle your rules

@Wayne Harrison

Why have a vote about your game? Do it however you like. It's not a competition.

@Nancy Niemczyk 
I quite frequently use Perplexity AI to look up things I don't know, especially pop culture. For this reason I never post my times here, as some might consider it "cheating", though to me, cheating is only relevant when there's competition, and I'm not competing with anyone.
Out of curiosity — why use AI and all the associated water/electricity when it sounds like a Google search would work just as well?

@Wayne Harrison
Yes, that is cheating-in my book.

@Wayne Harrison

There's no such thing as cheating in a single player game. Have fun!

@Wayne Harrison
I guess we all have our own rules.
Mine is: if I'm really stuck someplace and can't get a crossing toehold, I can google the names of actors. 
Very occasionally a geographic name.

Once filled, if not solved, I go through the whole puzzle once to flyspeck. Once. Then I hit Autocheck to find offending square(s). Blue stars don't bother me.

U do U!

@Lauren
Unless you change your settings, Google's first answer is now pretty much always either AI slop or ads. So it's no help in avoiding AI. I use DuckDuckGo's NOAI version.

@Charles Peterson

Similar to my approach. I'll also Google sports things or use a map to suss out a geographical answer. The searching is part of the fun. Which of these ports could it be, hmm?
@Ellen from Philly - This New Englander was grinning from ear to ear (and I’m originally from Philly) 😊

@Ellen from Philly

@John

@John On an analog clock, the little hand points to the hour and the big hand to minute (min for short).

This Alison answerde, "Who is ther
That knokketh so? I warante it a theef.
 “Why, nay," quod he, "God woot, my sweete leef,
I am thyn Absolon, my deerelyng.
“Of gold," quod he, "I have thee broght a ryng.
My mooder yaf it me, so God me save
Ful fyn it is, and therto wel ygrave.
This wol I yeve thee, if thou me kisse

….

For al his kepyng and his jalousye,
And Absolon hath kist hir nether ye,
And Nicholas is scalded in the towte.
This tale is doon, and God save al the rowte!

Matt.

@RichardZ @Matt @Nanu nanu
I disagree. I am an AVID fan of xword puzzles. I RAVE about them.

@RozzieGrandma

The weakness of this defense of the clue begins with comparing the adjective AVID to the verb "rave."  Already not interchangeable or conditionally mutually suggestible since they're different parts of speech.

And so, "raving" as the gerund of the verb "rave" has nothing to do definitionally with the adjective "raving."

Sorry.

Now, if you were just being tongue in cheek, I've already shut up so you don't have to tell me to.

@RichardZ
Clues.
Not definitions.

Clues.

@Matt
I'm a raving fan of crosswords.

@Charles Peterson

Can you imagine as a kid going on a treasure hunt where all the "clues" were merely definitions? I would have felt so cheated.

There needs to be some overlap, but that overlap can be pretty small. Especially for a late-week clue.

@RichardZ

Since our chief clue defender isn't defending it, it must be pretty bad.
@Caz I’m 43 and I was certain the big hand was the hour hand too for that reason but now I’m not sure what to believe. This feels like a Mandela Effect and I’m questioning my sanity.

userLocation: Hong Kong sometimes; Portland this month
commentBody:
@Caz

I for one have always referred to them the other way, and I’ve never heard anyone else do otherwise.  Sorry!

But, similar to you, I vastly prefer analogue clocks,   The hand positions convey information that must be synthesised from a digital display.

Mark (as I write this, the big hand is on the 2 and the little hand is on the 10)
@Caz I agree with you. Absolutely everyone I know uses “big hand” for hour. Now I’m wondering where this isn’t the norm.
@Caz    Yep, pretty much.  Sorry.

@Caz

When I think "big hand," I think length, not width. 
Hence, it's the minute hand.
(And I believe that's the accepted measure.)
@Laurie I'm wondering how this could EVER be a norm when the hour hand is physically smaller, haha

@Caz, I teach third grade, and that includes telling time. The SMALL hand indicates the hour, and the BIG hand, which actually touches the numbers, indicates minutes. With the big hand touching the numbers, the minutes are easier to count.

I've never heard your version and can only imagine it would be confusing to a child. On any watch or clock I've ever had, neither hand was thicker/wider than the other.
@Laurie, I'm so sorry, but I've NEVER seen or heard this, and I teach children to tell time! This makes absolutely zero sense. The had that is LONGER is the big hand. 🤷🏼‍♀️

@sotto voce, you are 100% correct. This isn't even open to discussion; the other version, no matter how many use it, is wrong and makes zero sense. 

I'm rarely categorical, but as an elementary school teacher, this is a hill I will die on!

@Susan E

On many - if not most - clocksand watches I've seen the hour hand was wider, but also shorter, than the minute hand. Do an image search for "clock" to see what I mean.

@Andrzej, I will look; I'm sure I've seen some old-fashioned, fancier clocks like that!

But my main point is that the big hand is for minutes!

@Susan E

The problem is the ambiguity of "big". Imagine two people - one short but obese, the other tall and thin: who is the big one of the two?
@Caz  When the big hand is on the 12 and little hand is on the 3 it is 3:00, not 12:15. That's how I've always used it.

@Laurie
That doesn't make sense. The minute hand is bigger. I would love to hear of a person who thinks that it is 1:30 when the big/larger/longer hand is on the 1, and the small hand is on the 6.

@Caz

Thank you, I was feeling very alone! Hour hand = big hand 4eva lol

@Susan E

I'm pretty sure I was taught the other way around and it made sense to me. Wider and "larger" number (the hour is the big number when comparing hours and minutes)

@Laurie

 It isn't the norm anywhere as far as I know.

@Caz

Old joke: 

Time announcement on a joint base:

Army and Air Force personnel, it is currently 1700.
Navy personnel, it is two bells.
And for all you Marines out there, the big hand is on the ....

(With apologies to any Marines, who I always want to be my friends.)

@Caz
The LONG HAND goes faster (as minutes go faster than hours)
But I do understand the confusion.
There is always that joke:  "The time is fifteen forty hours.  And for you Marines, the big hand is at...."
Army kid.  Sorry, no offense intended, gyrenes.

@sotto voce
Sotto-voce is sensible, precise, and correct!!
I can attest to it, having met her In Real Life.
Case closed.

I am finding this discussion surreal.

No! The 'big hand' is the longer minute hand. Always has been. 

Minute hand is always longer.
Hour hand is NOT always wider.

This hill I will die on.

@Caz Your husband loves you but yes, you are wrong. For those of you who still believe stuff you find on the internet, here's a website: https://tutors.com/lesson/how-to-tell-time-read-an-analog-clock Google vomits up a ton of slop but this seems to be genuine.

@lucky13

Really? I always thought I would love to be able to complete such a race. Can you imagine how incredible it must feel to do that? Such a challenge, aced? Also, I know some athletes, and yes, they devote their whole life to being the best they can be. Their dedication and work ethic are amazing, beyond anything I am capable of. I have a huge respect for them, and I wish I were more like them.

@lucky13 a lot of it comes down to whether or not you can afford to spend entire days in training. Most of us with a 9-5 cannot devote this much time and money solely to this endeavor, and I imagine it's even less likely for blue collar workers to be able to find the time and money required. The training schedule is very intensive, packed to the brim, with recovery and nutrition optimized down to the minutes and calories. There are no poor people competing in ironman events.

@Pat-a-pon

You do have a point, but dedication is just as important as the resources at your disposal. I have loads of free time, I'm financially comfortable and I love climbing, yet I've platteaued at a certain route difficulty. It would take training beyond what I'm comfortable with to progress further.

@lucky13 
One time in Hawaii I spotted a custom license plate: SWBKRN. It took me a moment to parse it, then I thought, "Ah, a triathlete!"

@lucky13

I’m with you.
I’m allergic to exercise.

So years ago, 
I asked a friend why she loved
training and competing in 
marathons and the Ironman.

Her father, brother and sister 
all had muscular dystrophy.

Her answer-
“Because I *can*!”

*she chose that career path*

Doh

@Andrzej

.,,and the sweat
and the sore muscles
and the old joints
and the sweat
and the boredom
and the agony
and the sweat

and the boredom…

@CCNY

That's why I like climbing - nothing about it is boring. Figuring out how to do the route is fun in itself, and being able to manage it physically is very satisfying. Also, it forces you to concentrate fully, making you forget about everything else. It's just awesome.

@lucky13,
I once met someone who had done the Ironman in Hawaii (its original location) and trained entirely using a treadmill (for running), a stationary bike (for cycling), and did his swim training in a lap pool. He was from Tokyo, which made the training regimen somewhat more understandable. I cannot imagine!

plural clues are quite often helpful
because of the assumed S.

Today?
No S.

Zero esses.

Twas S-less.

but many are much easier solved

There are also posts on here saying the supposedly easy puzzles were tough for them, and while some like to write those posts off as those commenters not being as smart as skilled as they are, that seems like a pretty narrow and self-serving way to look at things.)

Skeptical1,
Folks recommending your comment may be new to crosswords, but you are not. You should know that a crossword clue and answer are fine if there is *some* overlap in meaning; they need not be, and often are not, synonyms. The overlapping meanings are clear in the dictionary cites in the links.

My stumbling blocks?
I'm glad you asked.

STRIvE vs. STRIFE
RAKE IT IN

@Katie I see comments like this a lot. I was curious about what would happen if I did a random puzzle from a past Thursday.

I did June 8, 2017. It was still under my Thursday average, and it took me 2 and a half minutes longer than today's. I was able to finish it with no lookups or flyspecking.

Interestingly, there were comments expressing that it was tougher than the usual Thursday puzzle.

I didn't find it as difficult as many have implied the older ones were, but of course, one puzzle doesn't mean anything signficant.

@Katie

@Rich in Atlanta As promised - a Friday from July 13, 2018 by Trenton Charlson. At the very top of the puzzle were three straightforwardly clued answers: RAZZ, PIZZA and FIZZ. And then the 'reveal' near the bottom of the puzzle: "Rock band whose name is suggested by the first row of this puzzle." ZZTOP Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/13/2018&h=52a ....

@Steve L 

If those people are actually figuring out how to put the cube back to its original arrangement, I'd agree it's a feat in logic and spatial reasoning. However, I believe that the people who compete for "solving" times, have really simply memorized a procedure worked out by someone else for returning the cube to the original state. Because of this, I would consider what they're doing to be more a matter of manual dexterity than of logic. MHO

Nancy,
If your FLIGHT is not DIRECT, it will have several legs.
("Leg" as a segment of a journey).
@Nancy not every book suits every person. But Jane Eyre was my all-time favorite book until I read East of Eden. (Since then my favorites continue to evolve)
@Barry Ancona -- As I understand it, a non-stop flight has no stops, but a DIRECT flight doesn't change planes, but may stop one or more times to refuel or pick up passengers

@Nancy

Jane Eyre was the first liberated woman. She stood by her morals and principles no matter what occurred. She was determined to make something of herself, and not listen to gossip; she would see for herself. Her bravery, despite her fright at being alone in the dark with a wild person nearby, is a sign of her inner strength. Her resolve to not be a burden on anyone, not even new-found relatives, is further proof of her determination to be her own person. And her loyalty knows no bounds. She is, for sure, a bit too religious for me, but that is how her creator was raised, and therefore, she was as well. 

On top of that, the writing of her story is beautiful. The prose is economical (as opposed to, for instance, Victor Hugo, who can devote an entire chapter to the kind of flora in a churchyard. You may skip that chapter, class.)

But of course, we all have our favorites. I think I have about ten. Jane lives on every device I own; I can read her at any point, wherever I am stuck waiting, like a dr's office. It's good to have friends who don't talk back.

Lewis,
The correct term is non-stop, but "in common usage" (as per the clue) DIRECT is used to mean non-stop.

After our recent experience on a trip via DFW (nightmare) we are dedicating any future plans to involve DIRECT FLIGHTs even if we have to drive to a different city's airport....

@Mean Old Lady

It's always worthwhile to seek out a smaller airport that you may never have heard of. For instance, I am in PA, and most people use either Philadelphia or Newark of even LaGuardia to get to this area. But cute little LVIA (Lehigh Valley International Airport, aka ABE) is here too... that's Allentown... and while it doesn't go everywhere, it goes a lot of "where" and you can make other connections from there. It's only 20 minutes from my home, while it could be two hours in traffic to the other. I know it's growing and I rue the day it adds to its footprint, but for now, it's perfect.

@Mean Old Lady a few years ago New Orleans finally added its first British Airways transatlantic flight to Heathrow. A long flight, but so much better than having to change planes in some huge airport like Atlanta. It was originally 3 times a week, but is so popular that they recently added a fourth flight.
